coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of tiny carbon dots with high quantum yield using multi-walled carbon nanotubes as support for selective “turn-off-on” detection of rutin and Al3+
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Huiyu
Xu, Yuan
Zhao, Li
Ding, Jie
Chen, Mengyang
Chen, Guangrui
Li, Yi
Ding, Lan
coredata.dc:description: The solid-phase pyrolysis method is widely used in the synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) because of its large scale and solvent-saving advantages. However, CDs are inclined to agglomerate during the synthesis process, resulting in low quantum yield (QY) and inhomogeneous distribution of particle sizes. Here, a simple and effective solid dispersion surface pyrolysis method was used for the synthesis of tiny-carbon dots (T-CDs) with a high QY. Multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) were used as a support and 2-(N-morpholino)ethanesulfonic acid (MES) as a precursor. MES was uniformly dispersed on the surface of MWCNTs by hydrogen bonding and π-π interactions. Due to the excellent thermal conductivity and rigidity of MWCNTs, the precursor is carbonized without agglomeration. Compared with the CDs obtained by the direct pyrolysis method without the use of a solid support, the particle size of the T-CDs is small (0.69 ± 0.14 nm) and the QY increased from 17.8 to 42.8%. It is anticipated that this method will lead to the improvement of the QY of CDs. Applications of the T-CDs included fluorescence detection of rutin in pharmaceuticals, human urine, and human serum samples, and determination of Al3+ in tap water samples.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence detection of rutin and Al3+
Detection of rutin and Al3+ in samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ministry of Science and Technology of Jilin Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology of Jilin Province

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Fund of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We would like to express our appreciation to professor Harry Bennett Davis ( Oregon Health and Science University, Portland, OR, USA ) for his contribution to the promotion and revision of this manuscript. Also, this work was supported by the Open Project of State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment , Harbin Institute of Technology (No. QAK201809 ), Development Program of Ministry of Science and Technology of Jilin Province, China (No. 20180201011GX ), and the National Natural Science Fund of China (No. 51778175 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthetic route of T-CDs (A) and R-CDs (B), respectively. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
SEM images of MES (A), MWCNTs (B), MES/MWCNTs composites (C), and simple mixed product of MWCNTs and MES (D).
Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms (A) and pore size distributions (B) of MWCNTs and MES/MWCNTs composites. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) FT-IR spectra of MWCNTs (a), MES/MWCNTs composites (b) and MES (c). Temperature-dependent FT-IR spectra region of MWCNTs (B), MES (C), and MES/MWCNTs composites (D). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) Integrated fluorescence intensity of quinine sulfate, T-CDs and R-CDs with different absorbance. TEM images of T-CDs (B), T-CDs/MWCNTs composites (C), and R-CDs (D). Size distribution of T-CDs (E) and R-CDs (F) measured by DLS. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
XRD pattern (A) and FT-IR spectra (B) of the T-CDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
XPS full survey of T-CDs and R-CDs (A), XPS high-resolution survey of C1s (B), O1s (C), N1s (D) and S2p (E) of T-CDs (above) and R-CDs (below). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) UV-vis absorption (a), fluorescence excitation (b) and fluorescence emission (c) spectrum of the T-CDs, inset: photograph of the T-CDs under visible and UV light (365 nm) illumination. (B) Fluorescence spectra of the T-CDs recorded at excitation wavelengths from 300 to 460 nm. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) Fluorescence spectra of the T-CDs in the presence of different amounts of rutin, inset: logarithmic relationship between the fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0/F) of the T-CDs and the concentration of rutin. (B) Fluorescence spectra of the T-CDs/rutin system in the presence of different amounts of Al3+, inset: linear relationship between fluorescence enhancement (F-F0) of the T-CDs/rutin system and the concentration of Al3+. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) Fluorescence lifetimes of the T-CDs in the presence and absence of rutin. (B) UV-vis spectra of rutin in the presence and absence of the T-CDs. (C) Zeta potentials of the T-CDs, rutin and Al3+. (D) UV-vis spectra of Al3+, rutin and rutin after the addition of Al3+. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Recovery results for the determination of rutin in tablets, human urine and human serum samples.
Recovery results for the determination of Al3+ in water samples.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of tiny carbon dots with high quantum yield using multi-walled carbon nanotubes as support for selective “turn-off-on” detection of rutin and Al3+

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Jilin University, 2699 Qianjin Street, Changchun 130012, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment, Harbin Institute of Technology, 202 Haihe Road, Nangang District, Harbin 150090, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The solid-phase pyrolysis method is widely used in the synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) because of its large scale and solvent-saving advantages. However, CDs are inclined to agglomerate during the synthesis process, resulting in low quantum yield (QY) and inhomogeneous distribution of particle sizes. Here, a simple and effective solid dispersion surface pyrolysis method was used for the synthesis of tiny-carbon dots (T-CDs) with a high QY. Multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) were used as a support and 2-(N-morpholino)ethanesulfonic acid (MES) as a precursor. MES was uniformly dispersed on the surface of MWCNTs by hydrogen bonding and π-π interactions. Due to the excellent thermal conductivity and rigidity of MWCNTs, the precursor is carbonized without agglomeration. Compared with the CDs obtained by the direct pyrolysis method without the use of a solid support, the particle size of the T-CDs is small (0.69 ± 0.14 nm) and the QY increased from 17.8 to 42.8%. It is anticipated that this method will lead to the improvement of the QY of CDs. Applications of the T-CDs included fluorescence detection of rutin in pharmaceuticals, human urine, and human serum samples, and determination of Al3+ in tap water samples.

ce:section.ce:para: As a new entry to the family of carbon nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted increasing attention and have found broad applications in the detection of various analytes. Currently, researchers have developed many methods for preparing CDs; these include, hydrothermal [1], microwave-assisted [2], ultrasonic [3], and solid-state pyrolysis methods [4]. Solid-state pyrolysis has been the most promising method for preparation of CDs because it can be carried out neat and with high product yields [5,6]. However, due to the lack of a dispersion medium, the obtained CDs often aggregate into larger particles at elevated temperatures [7]; this leads to non-uniform particle sizes and low quantum yields (QYs).

In order to inhibit particle aggregation of CDs in the solid-state pyrolysis process, the use of a solid template has been proposed [7,8]. Using mesoporous silica as the template, Kurdyukov's group allowed the precursor (APTES) to remain in contact for 3 h [9]. The composites were then carbonized in air at 250 °C for 5 h, after which the template was removed by HF etching. The resultant CDs had a narrow size distribution with a diameter of 3.3 ± 0.9 nm. Bourlinos and co-workers synthesized CDs by carbonizing 2,4-diaminophenol dihydrochloride in the presence of an ion-exchange NaY zeolite as the template [10]. In order to combine the precursor with the template, a two-step exchange process was required. Following carbonization, the zeolite matrix was removed by HF etching; CDs were obtained with a size range from 4 to 6 nm. However, the QY of the CDs was only about 0.1%. Zong's group exposed mesoporous silica (template) to a solution of citric acid (precursor) to construct the template/precursor composites [11]. The composites were carbonized at 300 °C for 2 h in air; after etching with 2 M NaOH solution at 40 °C for 24 h; the resultant CDs were obtained in a narrow size distribution (1.5–2.5 nm) with a QY of 23%. Although the diameter of the CDs prepared by these methods was relatively uniform, without exception, these methods all involve the laborious preparation of a precursor-template composite and etching of the template after carbonization. Moreover, the QY of the CDs prepared by these methods is low.

More recently, Xu's group demonstrated an easier way to prepare CDs with a slightly large size distribution (2.5–5.2 nm) by using furfuryl alcohol as the precursor and mesoporous silica as the template [12]. The carbon replica (C/SBA-15) of SBA-15 template was prepared via pre-heating to 50 °C and 90 °C for 24 h, respectively, and annealing at 850 °C for 4 h. Following this regimen, CDs were obtained by a HNO3 vapor cutting method. The advantage with this method is that CDs can be directly isolated by in-situ filtration with water.

Herein, we describe a simple solid dispersion surface pyrolysis (SDSP) method that was developed for the synthesis of tiny carbon dots (T-CDs) by employing multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) as a support and 2-(N-morpholino)ethanesulfonic acid (MES: C6H13NO4S) as a precursor. This method proved to yield CDs with a uniform dispersed particle size and a stable morphology. MWCNTs were employed as the support medium due to their large surface area, good mechanical strength, high thermal stability, and their ability to adsorb appropriate organic precursors via hydrogen bonding and π-π interactions. MES was chosen as a precursor because of the presence of specific functional groups (S=O, −OH) which facilitate good hydrogen bonding and π-π interactions between it and the MWCNTs, and its insolubility in methanol. MES was uniformly dispersed on the surface of MWCNTs by a simple grinding method via hydrogen bonding and π-π interactions. After carbonization, the T-CDs were dissolved in methanol and isolated by centrifugation. Compared with the referenced CDs obtained by the direct pyrolysis method (R-CDs), the T-CDs have a uniform particle size and achieve a high QY at the same time.

In the past decades, analytical techniques including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [13], high-performance liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS/MS) [14], and capillary electrophoresis [15] were effectively used for rutin monitoring. Although these methods have advantages of high sensitivity and selectivity, they require trained personnel and sophisticated and expensive instruments. Compared with the currently developed rutin detection methods including sequential injection analysis [16], electrochemistry [17], UV-vis spectrometry [18], and room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) [19], fluorescence assays [20] with high sensitivity, easy operation and rapid response have received great attention. However, most of the fluorophore-based sensors including dyes [21] and semiconductor quantum dots [22] involve toxic hazardous reagents and heavy metal elements, which limit their practical application. CDs exhibit advantages of low toxicity, outstanding optical properties, and good biocompatibility hold great promise in the detection of rutin [23]. In this manuscript, the T-CDs can serve as a selective fluorescent probe for rutin and the determination of Al3+ is achieved due to the chelation between rutin and Al3+.

MWCNTs were acquired from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). MES was obtained from Gen-view Scientific Inc. (Texas, USA). Disodium phosphate dodecahydrate (Na2HPO4⋅12H2O) and sodium phosphate monobasic dihydrate (NaH2PO4⋅2H2O) were purchased from Tianjin Guangfu Institute of Fine Chemicals (Tianjin, China). Methanol (CH3OH), hydrochloric acid (HCl) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were purchased from Beijing Chemical Factory (Beijing, China). Rutin was purchased from Shanghai Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Ultrapure water (18 MΩ cm) used throughout the experiment was prepared with the use of a Milli-Q ultrapure water system (Millipore, USA). A stock solution of rutin (500 mg L−1) was prepared with ultrapure water. A stock solution of Al3+ (500 μM) was prepared by dissolving Al(NO3)3 in ultrapure water. Phosphate buffer solutions (PBS) of different pH values were prepared by dissolving Na2HPO4⋅12H2O, NaH2PO4⋅2H2O in ultrapure water, then titrating with either, HCl or NaOH to obtain the desired pH.

Solid-state pyrolysis was performed with the use of a YFK60 × 400/12Q-GC furnace (Shanghai Y-feng Electrical Furnace Co., Ltd, China). Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images were recorded on a JEM-6700F microscope (JEOL, Japan) and a JSM-6510 microscope (JEOL, Japan). Transmission election microscope (TEM) images were obtained by a JEM-2100 microscope (JEOL, Japan) and a JEM-2100F microscope (JEOL, Japan) using carbon coated copper grid (Xinxing Bairui, China) as substrate. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) and zeta potentials were measured by a Zetasizer Nano-ZS instrument (Malvern instruments, UK) with a 633 nm laser and a fixed scattering angle of 173° at 25 °C. The Z-average diameter, polydispersity index (PDI), and zeta potential values were acquired from three runs (each one with ten cycles). A Nicolet 360 FT-IR spectrometer (Wisconsin, USA) was used to obtain FT-IR spectra. The temperature-dependent FT-IR spectra were measured by a VERTEX 80v spectrometer (Bruker Optics, Germany). UV-vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were acquired with a Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) and an F-2700 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan), respectively. XRD patterns were obtained with the use of a Shimadzu XRD-6100 spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). XPS data were obtained with the use of an ESCALAB250 electron spectrometer (Thermo Electron, USA). Fluorescence lifetime experiments were obtained with a FLS 920 spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK). The concentrations of Al3+ were measured by an inductively coupled plasma-atomic emission spectroscopy (ICP-AES, Thermo Scientific iCAP 7000 series, England).

T-CDs were synthesized by a simple SDSP method. MES (0.6 g) was dispersed uniformly on MWCNTs (0.3 g) by grinding thoroughly for 3 min in an agate mortar to obtain the MES/MWCNTs composites. The MES/MWCNTs composites were transferred into a porcelain boat (3 cm long, 1.5 cm wide) and placed at the middle of a quartz tube in a furnace, and heated at 300 °C for 2 h under a nitrogen atmosphere. After cooling to room temperature, the dark brown product was dissolved in methanol and centrifuged (10000 rpm, 10 min). The used MWCNTs contained in the sediment were discarded. The supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane and further dialyzed against water in a dialysis membrane (molecular weight cut-off: 500 Da) for 8 h. The purified T-CDs solution was concentrated by rotary evaporation and dried at 60 °C to obtain T-CDs powder with a yield of 15%. Finally, an aqueous solution of T-CDs (0.09 mg mL−1) was prepared and stored at 4 °C.
R-CDs were carbonized under the same conditions without the presence of MWCNTs as a support.

The QYs of the T-CDs were measured by using quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 aqueous solutions as the reference (QY = 0.54). Aqueous solutions of different concentrations of quinine sulfate and T-CDs were prepared. UV absorption and integrated fluorescence intensity at 360 nm were recorded for each solution. The QY of the T-CDs can be calculated from the following equation:
Φ
x
=
Φ
s t
(
Κ
x
/
Κ
s t
)
(
η
x
/
η
s t
)
2
where K is the slope of the integrated fluorescence intensity-absorption curve and η is the refractive index of the solvent. The subscripts “x” and “st” refer to the sample and standard. Because the refractive indexes of aqueous 0.1 M H2SO4 and water are both 1.33, the fraction
η
x
/
η
s t
= 1. In order to minimize any re-absorption effects, UV absorption intensities at 360 nm were kept below 0.05.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of rutin and Al3+
ce:section.ce:para: Solutions containing 100 μL of the T-CDs solution (0.09 mg mL−1), 100 μL of PBS (200 mM, pH 6), and 600 μL of serial dilutions of rutin solution were mixed thoroughly, and diluted to 2 mL using ultrapure water. The solutions were allowed to stand for 3 min to achieve equilibrium, after which the fluorescence spectra were recorded.

For analysis of Al3+, 100 μL of the T-CDs solution (0.09 mg mL−1), 100 μL of PBS (200 mM, pH 6) and 600 μL of rutin solution (433.3 mg L−1) were mixed thoroughly. After equilibration for 3 min, varying concentrations of Al3+ were added to the T-CDs/rutin solutions and diluted to 2 mL using ultrapure water. Following a 5 min incubation period, the fluorescence spectra were recorded.

All the fluorescence spectra were recorded under the same conditions: the slit widths of the excitation and emission were set at 10 nm, and the fluorescence spectra were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The fluorescence intensity of the maximum emission peak was used for quantification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of rutin and Al3+ in samples
ce:section.ce:para: Rutin tablets (Shanxi Yunpeng Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd, China) were purchased from a local pharmacy (Changchun, China). The tablets were ground in an agate mortar to obtain a homogeneous powder, and then dissolved in ultrapure water to obtain a solution of 1 mg mL−1. The solution was sonicated for 30 min at room temperature, after which the solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane before further analysis. Fresh human urine and serum samples were obtained from healthy volunteers and diluted 10-fold with ultrapure water, respectively. Urine and serum samples were filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane filter for fluorescence detection. Tap water samples were obtained from three different taps on the Jilin University campus (Changchun, China). The water samples were filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane filter. Finally, the pretreated rutin tablets, human urine, human serum, and tap water samples were analyzed by the detection method described in part 2.5.

MWCNTs are an attractive support for carbon precursors due to their large surface area, excellent mechanical properties, and high thermal conductivity. MWCNTs have a huge number of C=C and −OH groups on their surface, which enables them to form hydrogen bonding and π-π interactions with appropriate precursors. MES was selected as a precursor because the molecule contains abundant functional groups (S=O, −OH, etc.) and it is sparingly soluble in methanol. Fig. 1 shows a schematic diagram of the preparation procedure of the T-CDs and the R-CDs.

Varying the temperature and time for pyrolysis as well as the mass ratio of MES and MWCNTs led to significantly different outcomes. As the pyrolysis temperature was increased from 150 to 300 °C, the QY of the T-CDs increased from 17.0% to 42.8% (Fig. S1A). However, heating beyond 300 °C led to a decline in QY. It was also found that the QY of the T-CDs increased with the extension of pyrolysis time from 60 to 120 min (Fig. S1B). When the time was increased to more than 120 min, the QY of the T-CDs decreased. It was found that the best QY was obtained when the mass ratio of MES and MWCNTs was 2:1 (Fig. S1C). Based on these observations, the optimum experimental conditions were a pyrolysis temperature of 300 °C, a pyrolysis time of 2 h, and a MES:MWCNT mass ratio of 2:1.

The first step in the experimental procedure involved mechanical mixing of the MES and MWCNTs to make a homogeneous mixture. It can be seen from the SEM images that MES exhibits an irregular block structure (Fig. 2 A) and MWCNTs (Fig. 2B) show a curved tubular structure. After fully ground, the original bulk MES disappears, and leads to an increase in the diameter of MWCNTs, indicating the uniform dispersion of MES on the surface of MWCNTs and the successful formation of the MES/MWCNTs composites (Fig. 2C). Conversely, the structure of MES can be clearly distinguished in the SEM image of the simple mixed product of MWCNTs and MES (Fig. 2D). MWCNTs and MES/MWCNTs composites display similar nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms in Fig. 3 A, indicating the original pore structure of the MWCNTs is preserved after the MES dispersion. As shown in the pore size distribution curves in Fig. 3B, the most probable pore diameter of MWCNTs decreased from 39.2 to 35.6 nm due to the dispersion of MES on the surface of MWCNTs [24].

The FT-IR spectra demonstrate the intermolecular interactions between MWCNTs and MES (Fig. 4 A). MWCNTs exhibit three distinguishable peaks at 3431, 1621 and 1107 cm−1, which are attributed to the stretching vibration of O−H, C=C and C−C groups, respectively. After MES dispersion, the C=C vibration of the MES/MWCNTs composites is located at 1606 cm−1; this shift is attributed to the presence of π-π interactions between MES and MWCNTs [25–27]. Fig. 4 shows temperature-dependent FT-IR spectra in the 3700-2400 cm−1 region of MWCNTs, MES, and MES/MWCNTs composites with an increment of 25 °C (25–225 °C). The absorption spectra of MWCNTs remain basically unchanged with an increase of temperature (Fig. 4B). Peak at 3460 cm−1 is attributed to the hydrogen-bonded hydroxyl groups of MES (Fig. 4C). The peak shows an intensity decrease and shifts to a higher wavenumber with increasing temperature. As seen in the absorption spectra of the MES/MWCNTs composites (Fig. 4D), the strong hydrogen-bonded hydroxyl vibration of MES centered at 3460 cm−1 disappears. An additional broad absorption peak at 3277 cm−1 is observed. With increasing temperature, the peak shifts to 3319 cm−1 with a decrease in absorption. These results indicate the formation of hydrogen bonds between MES and MWCNTs [28–31].

When the MES/MWCNTs composites are pyrolyzed, carbonization between MES molecules takes place on the surface of MWCNTs. After they are formed, further agglomeration of the T-CDs is prevented because the T-CDs are absorbed on the surface of MWCNTs which inhibits their migration. In this way, ultra-small T-CDs with uniform particle size distribution are formed with high QY. The advantage of this method is illustrated by comparison with the QY and particle size distribution of the R-CDs. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the QYs determined using quinine sulfate as the reference are 42.8% and 17.8% for the T-CDs and the R-CDs, respectively. As shown in TEM images (Fig. 5B and D), the T-CDs produced by the SDSP method display a smaller and more uniform diameter compared with the R-CDs. For comparison, the images of blank carbon coated copper grid and the T-CDs/MWCNTs composites are displayed in Fig. S2 and Fig. 5C. More than 500 particles were choosen to calculate average diameter of T-CDs and R-CDs by using Nano Measurer 1.2 developed by Department of Chemistry, Fudan University (http://nano-measurer.software.informer.com/). The average diameters of T-CDs and R-CDs are 0.69 ± 0.14 and 1.80 ± 0.64 nm, respectively (inset in Fig. 5B and D). Additionally, DLS measurements reveal that the average hydrodynamic diameter of the T-CDs and the R-CDs are 0.98 nm (PDI = 0.023) and 2.45 nm (PDI = 0.124), respectively (Fig. 5E and F). This result is consistent with the TEM because hydrodynamic diameter is usually slightly larger than the dry-state diameter [32]. The mean diameter of the R-CDs is generally 1–2 nm larger than that of the T-CDs. We believe that this aggregation is responsible to the relatively low QY of the R-CDs.

The analysis of XPS (Fig. 7) reveals that there is no significant difference in types of chemical bonds within the T-CDs and the R-CDs. However, the high-resolution C1s XPS spectrum of the T-CDs shows a relatively-larger peak associated with C-C/C=C bonds compared to that of the R-CDs (Fig. 7B). These results suggest that a higher QY is associated with formation of the product with the largest number of carbon-carbon bonds. To test this hypothesis, l-cysteine (Cys), ammonium citrate (AC), and ascorbic acid (AA) all capable of forming hydrogen bonding and π-π interactions with MWCNTs were used as precursors. The results showed that the QY of CDs synthesized by the SDSP method using MWCNTs as a support is higher than that from direct carbonization under the same temperature and time (Fig. S3A). High resolution C1s spectra of the CDs synthesized using these precursors are shown in Figs. S3B–D. The peak at 284.4 eV is assigned to C−C/C=C. The spectra of the three CDs synthesized using MWCNTs as a support show larger C-C/C=C peaks compared with those synthesized from direct carbonization. These results indicate that the presence of MWCNTs is responsible for the high content of carbon-carbon bonds and the high QY.

The XRD pattern of the T-CDs (Fig. 6 A) shows a broad diffraction peak at 24.5° corresponding to the randomness of carbon atoms [33]. The FT-IR spectrum of the T-CDs is presented in Fig. 6B. The broad absorption from 2957 to 3419 cm−1 is attributed to the overlap of the C−H, N−H and O−H stretching vibrations [34]. The absorption peak at 1626 cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching vibration of C=O [35]. Weak absorption peaks at 1512 and 1447 cm−1 indicate the presence of C=C and C−N groups [35,36]. Peaks at 1213 and 1038 cm−1 are attributable to the stretching vibrations of C−O and C−O−C, respectively [37]. The absorption peak at 1169 cm−1 can be assigned to C−N and C−S stretching vibrations [38]. The absorption peak at 735 cm−1 is related to the stretching vibration of C−S [39].

The XPS full scan spectrum (Fig. 7A) of the T-CDs and the R-CDs shows five main peaks at 531.3, 400.0, 285.0, 229.0 and 164 eV, which are ascribed to O1s, N1s, C1s, S2s and S2p signals, respectively [40]. Fig. 7B shows the high-resolution spectrum of C1s, which indicates the presence of C−C/C=C (284.5 eV), C−N (284.9 eV), C−S (285.8 eV) and C-O (286.5 eV) [40]. The high-resolution spectrum of O1s is shown in Fig. 7C, there are two peaks at 531.7 eV and 532.2 eV for C=O and C−O−C/C−OH. The high-resolution spectrum of N1s (Fig. 7D) contains two dominant peaks, which are ascribed to N−H (399.9 eV) and N−C3 (401.7 eV) [41]. In addition, The S2p XPS spectrum (Fig. 7E) shows two distinct peaks at 167.7 eV and 168.8 eV, respectively, which demonstrate the presence of −C−SO2− and −C−SO3− [42]. Taken together, the XPS and FT-IR results demonstrate that the T-CDs produced by the SDSP method contained abundant functional groups.

UV-vis absorption spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy were employed to explore the optical properties of the T-CDs. Fig. 8 A shows that the T-CDs have two UV absorption peaks centered at 284 and 345 nm, corresponding to π-π* and n-π* transitions associated with the C=C and C=O bonds, respectively [43]. The maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of T-CDs are at 360 and 427 nm, respectively. The T-CDs appear light yellow color in visible light and bright blue under UV light (365 nm) excitation (inset in Fig. 8A) due to fluorescence. Like most CDs, the emission wavelength of T-CDs red-shifts from 393 to 504 nm when the excitation wavelength is changed from 300 to 460 nm (Fig. 8B). Excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior is caused by different functional groups on the surface of the T-CDs [44]. Fluorescence properties of the T-CDs remained unchanged after storage in aqueous solution for 30 days (Fig. S4). In addition, the photostability and salt effects of the T-CDs were investigated (Figs. S5 and S6). The T-CDs exhibit good stability under continuous irradiation (360 nm) and the fluorescence intensity of T-CDs shows no obvious change even in the presence of 1 M NaCl.

To achieve maximum sensitivity, the pH and equilibrium time were optimized. As shown in Fig. S7, the fluorescence intensity of the T-CDs is stable over the entire pH range, but higher in acidic and neutral pH environments; this is attributed to the abundant functional groups at the surface of the T-CDs. The quenching efficiency (F0/F), where F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of the T-CDs in the absence and presence of rutin (50 mg L−1) as a function of pH was also investigated. The quenching efficiency reached a maximum at pH 6.0, and decreased at higher pH. This is due to the reaction of the −OH of rutin, which directly influences the interaction between rutin and the T-CDs [22]. Therefore, pH 6.0 was chosen for further experiments. As shown in Fig. S8, the fluorescence intensity of the T-CDs rapidly decreased upon addition of rutin, but stabilized within 3 min. The ultra-small particle size of the T-CDs and the great solubility of rutin in detection environment are responsible for the fast fluorescence response. Therefore, 3 min was selected as the equilibrium time in subsequent experiments.

Under the conditions described above, fluorescence “turn-off” detection of rutin was carried out. As shown in Fig. 9 A, the fluorescence intensity of the T-CDs decreased with the increasing concentrations of rutin. A plot of log F0/F vs concentration (c) of rutin in the range of 0.06–130 mg⋅L−1 yielded a straight line (R2 = 0.998) with a y intercept of 0.0117 (Eq. (1)).
(1) log (F0/F) = 0.0117c + 0.0117

This relationship represents the fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0/F) of the T-CDs and the concentration of rutin. The detection limit was 0.02 mg L−1 (3σ/k), where σ and k represent the standard deviation of blank measurements (n = 11) and the slope of the calibration curve, respectively. Table S1 summarizes the linear range and detection limit previously reported for rutin by other methods. The T-CDs have a wider linear range and comparatively lower detection limit than those methods.

Generally, rutin tablets contain additional substances which may interfere with the detection of rutin. Similarly, there are many substances in human urine and serum samples which may affect the fluorescence of the T-CDs. Therefore, studies were undertaken to study the fluorescence detection of rutin in the presence of these substances. As shown in Fig. S9, fluorescence of the T-CDs was quenched only by rutin, and the interfering substances had no measurable effect.

Rutin has a highly conjugated π system, and a spatial arrangement of O atoms, that allows its function as a chelating ligand for metal ions. It has been shown that rutin strongly chelates Al3+, this makes the T-CDs/rutin system well-suited for the detection of Al3+ [45]. Fluorescence measurements of a pure mixture of rutin-Al3+, and that of a T-CDs/rutin system in the absence and presence of Al3+ were determined. Rutin-Al3+ mixture had almost no fluorescence. However, the fluorescence of the T-CDs/rutin system was significantly restored after the addition of Al3+. The results demonstrate that the fluorescence of the T-CDs quenched by rutin can be recovered by addition of Al3+. Thus, detection of Al3+ by the T-CDs was achieved in the presence of the T-CDs/rutin complex.

The detection of Al3+ was strongly influenced by equilibrium time. As shown in Fig. S10, the fluorescence of the T-CDs/rutin system in the presence of Al3+ reached its maximum after equilibrium for 5 min. Therefore, 5 min was selected as the equilibrium time for Al3+ detection. As depicted in Fig. 9B, the fluorescence intensity of the T-CDs/rutin system gradually increased with increasing Al3+ concentration. A plot of F-F0 vs Al+3 concentration yielded a straight line (R2 = 0.9907) with an intercept of 6.8655 as indicated in Eq. (2).
(2) F-F0 = 8.2531c + 6.8655

Here F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of the T-CDs/rutin in the absence and presence of Al3+, respectively. The linear range of concentrations was found to be 0.4–70 μmol L−1 and the limit of detection was 0.1 μmol L−1, which is comparable to those obtained by other methods for Al3+ detection (Table S2).

In order to evaluate the selectivity of the T-CDs/rutin system, the variation in fluorescence intensity (F-F0) of the system was measured in the presence of various metal ions and substances, which may interfere with the detection of Al3+, including K+, Na+, Ni+, Ag+, Au+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Ba2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, F−, Cl−, I−, PO4 3−, NO3 −, [Fe(CN)6]3-, MnO4 − and H2O2. As shown in Fig. S11, Al3+ restored the fluorescence of the T-CDs/rutin significantly, while no obvious change in F-F0 was observed for other interfering substances. This suggests that the detection system with the T-CDs/rutin system is highly selective.

Fluorescence quenching of CDs is usually caused by either static or dynamic quenching. Dynamic quenching is accompanied by a change in fluorescence lifetime, whereas static quenching involves no change of fluorescence lifetime. Fig. 10 A shows the fluorescent decay curves of the T-CDs in the absence and presence of rutin. The average fluorescence lifetimes of the T-CDs before and after the addition of rutin, 8.14 ns and 8.24 ns when fitted with a double-exponential function, are essentially unchanged. Basically unchanged lifetimes indicate a static quenching mechanism [46]. The interaction between rutin and the T-CDs was also characterized by UV-vis spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 10B, the UV-vis absorption spectrum for rutin is significantly increased in the presence of the T-CDs, which demonstrates an apparent interaction between rutin and T-CDs. The zeta potentials of rutin and the T-CDs were determined to be −14.1 ± 0.5 and +2.4 ± 0.2 mV, respectively (Fig. 10C). The opposite zeta potential between rutin and the T-CDs results in the electrostatic attraction between them, which further suggests the mechanism of fluorescence quenching is static quenching.

The mechanism of fluorescence recovery induced by Al3+ was also investigated. As depicted in Fig. 10D, the UV-vis spectrum of Al3+ shows no effective absorption. However, by introducing Al3+ to the aqueous solution of rutin, the two characteristic absorption peaks of rutin at 255 and 352 nm shift to 266 and 361 nm, and a new peak centered at 410 nm evolves. These results indicate that a new complex is formed by interaction of rutin with Al3+. The zeta potentials of Al3+ and rutin are +7.7 ± 0.3 and −14.1 ± 0.5 mV, respectively (Fig. 10C), indicating the existence of electrostatic attraction between Al3+ and rutin. The electrostatic attraction between Al3+ and rutin is greater than that between rutin and T-CDs. This suggests significant chelation between rutin and Al3+. The ability of Al3+ to chelate strongly to rutin causes the formation of an Al3+/rutin complex at the expense of the T-CDs/rutin complex. Consequently, the fluorescence of the T-CDs is restored.

In order to investigate the practical application of the method based on the T-CDs, the method was applied to the detection of rutin in commonly-available rutin tablets, human urine, and human serum samples. As shown in Table 1 , spiked recoveries of rutin are in the range of 96.8–102.6% with relative standard deviations lower than 2.6%, indicating the reliability of the method.

In addition, the method based on the T-CDs/rutin system was also applied to the detection of Al3+ in tap water samples. The results are summarized in Table 2 . Spiked tap water samples were prepared by standard addition of Al3+ solution at known concentration. Results obtained by the T-CDs/rutin system were consistent with the results obtained by ICP-AES, demonstrating that the system is applicable.

In summary, T-CDs were synthesized via a simple SDSP method using MES as a precursor and MWCNTs as a support. MES was uniformly dispersed onto the surface of MWCNTs by grinding, and the T-CDs were obtained after carbonization in a furnace. The prepared T-CDs exhibit a narrow size distribution and a high QY. The SDSP strategy using MWCNTs as a support provides a new and effective way to synthesize high-performance T-CDs with high QY and small size. Furthermore, the T-CDs with surprising properties may provide a structural explanation for the high QY of small-sized CDs and contribute to the study of the optical properties of small-sized CDs. In addition, a new “turn-off-on” method based upon the T-CDs, for detection of rutin and Al3+ in various samples was established.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We would like to express our appreciation to professor Harry Bennett Davis (Oregon Health and Science University, Portland, OR, USA) for his contribution to the promotion and revision of this manuscript. Also, this work was supported by the Open Project of State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment, Harbin Institute of Technology (No. QAK201809), Development Program of Ministry of Science and Technology of Jilin Province, China (No. 20180201011GX), and the National Natural Science Fund of China (No. 51778175).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2018.11.049.
